data.title: YPF investors to pursue international treaty arbitration in $16B judgment
data.description:
YPF (YPF) investors who saw their $16B U.S. judgment against Argentina thrown out on appeal last month have told the trial judge that they plan to pursue arbitration while continuing to contest the appeals court ruling, Bloomberg reported Wednesday.

A U.S. District Court awarded the plaintiffs the judgment in 2023, ruling that Argentina's government violated their rights in its 2012 nationalization of oil company YPF (YPF), but the U.S. Second Circuit Court of Appeals reversed the judgment on March 27, saying she misinterpreted Argentine law.

Litigation funder Burford Capital (BUR), which has been financing the lawsuit and would have taken a large share of the award, said after the ruling it could take the case to arbitration as well as appeal the case to the full Second Circuit or the U.S. Supreme Court.

Burford (BUR) shares plunged after last month's ruling and have since lost ~40%.

data.title: Amerisafe signals mid-single-digit premium growth amid soft pricing and rate pressure
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: AMERISAFE, Inc. (AMSF) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "We are pleased with our solid start to 2026, marked by continued growth, disciplined execution and attractive underwriting performance." (President, CEO, Principal Accounting & Financial Officer and Director G. Frost)
  • "During the quarter, we grew net premiums earned by 9%. We also delivered a combined ratio of 93.2% and produced operating earnings of $0.50 per share." (President and CEO Frost)
  • "The workers' compensation market remains competitive and continues to operate in a prolonged soft pricing environment amid persistent industry headwinds, such as claims severity and economic uncertainty." (President and CEO Frost)
  • "Retention for policies for which we offered renewal was 92.4% in the quarter, and pricing remains strong, helping offset continued downward pressure in filed loss costs." (Executive VP & Chief Risk Officer Vincent Gagliano)
  • "Net investment income decreased 0.8% to $6.6 million due to lower average investable assets." (President and CEO Frost)
  • "Next quarter, I'll have the pleasure of passing the financial remarks off to Guillermo Ramos, our new CFO." (President and CEO Frost)

Outlook

  • "We continue to see positive wage growth in our targeted classes of business, coming in at 4.5% for the quarter, while headcount change was essentially flat." (Executive VP Gagliano)
  • "We believe continued payroll growth across our targeted industries indicates relatively healthy business activity despite ongoing economic uncertainty." (Executive VP Gagliano)
  • "We're still looking at mid-single-digit decreases for the year" on loss costs, with filings in AMERISAFE’s five biggest states ranging "from down almost 9% to down 1.2%." (Executive VP Gagliano)
  • "Is it linear? No. But we're shooting for that mid-single-digit range, and we've been hitting that." (President and CEO Frost)

Financial Results

  • "Gross premiums written were $88.5 million" and "net earned premiums were $75.1 million in the quarter." (Executive VP Gagliano)
  • "The current accident year loss ratio was 72% for the quarter" and "we had $7.6 million or 10.1 points of favorable development in the quarter," resulting in "a net loss ratio of 61.9% for the quarter." (President and CEO Frost)
  • "Total underwriting and other expenses were $22.3 million for the quarter, resulting in an expense ratio of 29.7%." (President and CEO Frost)
  • "Net income was $8.1 million or $0.43 per diluted share, while operating net income was $9.5 million or $0.50 per diluted share." (President and CEO Frost)
  • "We repurchased nearly 120,000 shares of common stock" for "a total of $4 million" and said remaining authorization was "$12.9 million." (President and CEO Frost)

Q&A

  • Mark Hughes, Truist Securities: "how did you see inflation in the quarter?"; President and CEO Frost: "No marginal changes from what we talked about at year-end, Mark. Medical inflation is real. We are living it. We are reserving properly for it."
  • Mark Hughes, Truist Securities: "How about NCCI loss costs"; Executive VP Gagliano: "We're still looking at mid-single-digit decreases for the year" and cited a range "from down almost 9% to down 1.2%."
  • David Samar, Citizens JMP: "are there any certain industries or areas of the market that are driving growth"; President and CEO Frost: "it's been pretty steady across our book of business" and "it's been pretty prolific throughout the book of business."
  • Robert Edward Farnam, Brean Capital: "how does that compare to the duration of your liabilities?"; President and CEO Frost: "our average duration on our portfolio -- on our liabilities is between 3 and 4 years" and attributed it to "our high-touch model" to "close and settle these claims as quickly as we can."
  • Mark Hughes, Truist Securities: "how sustainable" is growth; President and CEO Frost: "I totally believe it is sustainable" and "we're shooting for that mid-single-digit range."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Slightly negative (analysts) with inflation, loss-cost decreases, and industry deterioration in focus, including "when you look at... accident years '24 and '25" and "pressure" questions around trend durability. (Mark Hughes, Truist; Robert Edward Farnam, Brean)
  • Neutral to slightly positive (management) with confidence anchored on pricing/risk selection, including "We remain confident in our strategy" and "committed to consistent execution." (President and CEO Frost)
  • Compared with last quarter, management again emphasized soft-market pressure, but Q1 discussion added a near-term positive data point: "no claims with incurred value over $1 million" in the current accident year quarter. (President and CEO Frost)

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Current-quarter growth pace moderated versus Q4: "$88.5 million" gross premiums written in Q1 2026 vs. "gross premium written grew 11.7%" in Q4 2025. (Executive VP Gagliano)
  • Retention eased: "92.4%" in Q1 2026 vs. "93.7%" in Q4 2025. (Executive VP Gagliano)
  • Large-claim commentary shifted from Q4 severity discussion ("25 claims with incurred value over $1 million") to Q1 quarter commentary of "no claims with incurred value over $1 million" in the quarter. (President and CEO Frost)
  • Capital and portfolio metrics were updated with a smaller cash and invested assets base: "approximately $774 million" at Q1 2026 quarter end vs. "roughly $797 million" at Q4 2025 quarter end. (President and CEO Frost)

Risks and Concerns

  • "Prolonged soft pricing environment" and "downward pressure in filed loss costs" were framed as ongoing constraints. (President and CEO Frost; Executive VP Gagliano)
  • "Continued rate pressure and general high claim severity" were cited as creating "modest upward pressure on the current accident year" loss ratio. (President and CEO Frost)
  • Medical inflation remained a key theme: "Medical inflation is real" and "continued pressure on medical inflation industry-wide." (President and CEO Frost)

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q1 as a continuation of disciplined, distribution-led premium expansion in a soft pricing market, pairing 9% net earned premium growth with a 93.2% combined ratio, while highlighting persistent rate/loss-cost pressure and medical severity inflation; leadership also signaled a CFO transition next quarter, continued share repurchases, and reiterated that growth is expected to remain “mid-single-digit” and “sustainable,” though not linear.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Northrop Grumman Systems wins $207M contract modification, lifting the value to $596M
data.description:
• Northrop Grumman Systems (NOC) has received a $207.9M contract modification for one more year of logistics support services.
  • With this move, the total contract value has increased from $388.1M to $596.0M.
  • The work under this contract is expected to continue until April 30, 2027.
  • At the time of award, $78.0M in FMS funds have been allocated, with the Air Force Life Cycle Management Center handling the contract.
  • On April 22, the stock closed slightly lower at ~$588.52 in after-hours.

data.title: General Dynamics unit wins $230M U.S. Navy contract modification
data.description:
• General Dynamics (GD) unit Bath Iron Works won a $230.5M contract modification for work on Arleigh Burke-class guided missile destroyers.
  • The contract covers planning, yard services, long-lead material support, and procurement of key ship components.
  • The work will be carried out in Bath, Maine, and is scheduled to be completed by July 2027.
  • If all contract options are fully exercised, the total contract value could reach $895M.
  • At the time of award, about $10.2M (4%) in Navy FY26 funds will be obligated, which will expire at the end of FY26.
  • The Supervisor of Shipbuilding, Conversion, and Repair, Bath, Maine, is the contracting activity.

data.title: Weatherford says Mexico starting to pay suppliers as oilfield activity normalizes
data.description:
Weatherford International (WFRD) said Wednesday that oilfield activity in Mexico has normalized and the company is starting to get paid ​after Pemex raised funds to pay its suppliers after long delays, Reuters reported.

Oilfield service companies had significantly reduced activities due to state-run Pemex's accumulation of billions of dollars in pending payments to ​contractors and providers in recent years.

"We are starting to get paid," and "we think that stability will continue on an activity level... So I ​think over the next few years, [Mexico] will be a bright spot," Weatherford (WFRD) CEO Girish Saligram said on the company's earnings conference call.

Weatherford (WFRD) received a large payment in Q4 2025 and another in Q1 from its main customer in Mexico, which still owed $283M as of March 31, CFO Anuj Dhruv said on the call, without identifying the customer by name.

The company also warned a hit to earnings from the Middle East will get worse this quarter, totaling $30M-$50M over this year's H1, Saligram said, based on oilfield activity in the region starting to normalize by the end of the current quarter.

Weatherford (WFRD) has been forced to pause operations for several weeks in countries including Iraq, Qatar, and parts of Kuwait, the CEO said, adding that a jump in freight costs, jet fuel, and trucking expenses also have caused strain.

Impacts from the war will show more clearly in Q2 after the company reported better-than-expected Q1 earnings, Saligram said, while noting a resolution to the conflict would mean oilfield services companies would be among the first to reap the benefits as countries rebuild their damaged energy infrastructure.

data.title: Tesla pares gain as investors factor in Elon Musk's warning of heavy capex spending
data.description:
Tesla (TSLA) pared its earnings rally in postmarket trading on Wednesday as investors gauged the company's Q1 earnings report and business update.

During the earnings conference call, CEO Elon Musk said 2026 will be a very exciting year. He said the company's heavy capital expenditure spending of about $25B this year will pay off in the future. Cybercab and Semi truck production will begin this year, although slowly at first, before going exponential at the end of the year and in 2027, per Musk. 

"There is a large pipeline of significant improvements for FSD, and it will be possible to achieve unsupervised FSD anywhere in the world where it is legally permitted," highlighted Musk. He also confirmed that the robotaxi service was rolled out to Houston and Texas.

On the humanoid robot front, Musk said Optimus will be useful outside of factories next year. The company will use the Fremont factory to produce Optimus 3 and is building a second Optimus factory at Giga Texas. It is anticipated that Tesla (TSLA) will hold an Optimus reveal event later in the year.

Musk said the company is already making plans for the AI6 chip. 

Weighing in on the earnings report, Seeking Alpha analyst Brett Ashcroft Green highlighted that the quarter had some interesting catalysts finally coming to the forefront, with Giga 1 now ramping up the Semi production. "To me, this was always the most interesting catalyst rather than robotaxis or Optimus. To eventually one day be able to create convoys of autonomous semi-trucks could be as revolutionary as the advent of the rail industry itself," he noted. "Combine this with a near doubling of paid robotaxi miles, and you probably have enough meat on the narrative bone to create renewed forward momentum," added Green.

Shares of Tesla (TSLA) were up 0.2% in the late session to $389.25 vs. the 52-week range of $244.43 to $98.83.

data.title: Earnings snapshot: ServiceNow tops $3.77B Q1 revenue, Non-GAAP EPS in line at $0.97
NBHC targets 2026 Q4 EPS above $1 as Vista integration drives ~10% loan growth and ~4% NIM outlook
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: National Bank Holdings Corporation (NBHC) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "The NBH team delivered an outstanding first quarter, and we believe we're well positioned to have a very strong year" (Founder, Chairman & CEO Tim Laney), adding: "momentum across the organization reinforces our belief in our ability to grow our earnings this year and surpass $1 of earnings per share in the fourth quarter."
  • "In the first quarter, we delivered record loan fundings and our net interest margin expanded to 4.06%" (CEO Laney), while also stating, "We experienced positive trends with all credit metrics."
  • "On an adjusted basis, we reported net income of $32.6 million or $0.72 of earnings per diluted share, 43% higher than the prior quarter" (Executive VP & CFO Nicole Van Denabeele), and "Fully taxable equivalent net interest income for the quarter totaled $111 million."
  • "During the first quarter, we closed the Vista acquisition, generated record quarterly loan originations of $805 million and delivered annualized loan growth of 12.4%" (CFO Van Denabeele), including "$285 million of organic loan growth" and "$1.9 billion of loans acquired in the Vista acquisition."
  • "We increased our quarterly dividend by 3% to $0.32 per share" and "restart[ed] our stock buyback program with $16 million purchased in Q1" (President Aldis Birkans).
  • "The platform has generated over 1,300 user applications year-to-date" for 2UniFi, with "weekly application volume accelerating from about 40 per week to most recently nearly 400" (CEO Laney), while adding, "we still have work to do to drive higher deposit account openings and loan fundings."

Outlook

  • "We expect to achieve our full year loan growth guidance of approximately 10%" (CFO Van Denabeele).
  • "For the remainder of 2026, we expect net interest margin to remain near 4%" (CFO Van Denabeele).
  • "For the remainder of 2026, we project to achieve our full year fee income guidance of $75 million to $80 million" (CFO Van Denabeele), with the outlook including "$2 million to $4 million of 2UniFi revenue, which we expect to be weighted towards the back half of the year."
  • "As previously guided, we project total noninterest expense for the full year 2026 to be in the range of $320 million to $330 million" (CFO Van Denabeele).
  • Guidance framing was consistent with last quarter’s positioning around Q4 2026 EPS, as management again stated it is "on track to deliver earnings in excess of $1 per share in the fourth quarter of 2026" (CFO Van Denabeele), after having used the same Q4 2026 target in Q4 2025.

Financial Results

  • "Loan balances increased by $2.2 billion or 29% during the quarter" (CFO Van Denabeele), alongside "Deposit balances increased by $2.2 billion during the quarter" and a period-end "loan-to-deposit ratio ended the quarter at 91.9%."
  • "Net interest margin expanded 17 basis points during the first quarter to 4.06%" (CFO Van Denabeele), and she attributed the expansion to "a 24 basis point increase in earning asset yields" and noted the quarter included "about 5 basis points of loan accretion addition from the Vista acquisition" (CFO Van Denabeele).
  • "We recorded $4 million of provision expense" (CFO Van Denabeele); "Net charge-offs were 8 basis points for the quarter" and "the allowance coverage ratio remained consistent at 1.18%" (CFO Van Denabeele).
  • "Noninterest income increased 16.9% year-over-year and totaled $18 million for the quarter" (CFO Van Denabeele), while "noninterest expense totaled $96.8 million for the quarter and included $15.3 million of acquisition and restructuring costs" (CFO Van Denabeele).
  • "Common equity Tier 1 ratio ended the quarter at 12.5%, and the total capital ratio was a strong 15.8%" (CFO Van Denabeele), and "Tangible book value per share was $26" (CFO Van Denabeele).

Q&A

  • Jeff Rulis, D.A. Davidson: Asked about pulling "$1" EPS forward into Q3; CEO Laney responded, "we feel very, very good about our momentum" and said Q1 growth "may very well be the tip of the iceberg," citing "the opportunity to expand treasury management services, wealth management services, residential banking services in markets like Dallas" (CEO Laney).
  • Jeff Rulis, D.A. Davidson: Pressed on why guide is "near 4%" margin after 4.06%; CFO Van Denabeele said Q1 had "about 5 basis points of loan accretion" and added, "we expect to fund that loan growth with full relationship core deposits" and "maintaining our strong cost of deposits under 2% that gets you right at a 4% margin" (CFO Van Denabeele).
  • Kelly Motta, KBW: Challenged whether ~10% loan-growth guidance implies slowdown after 12.4% Q1; CEO Laney said, "We don't typically make changes in year on guidance" and added, "we feel very good about our growth prospects this year" (CEO Laney).
  • Kelly Motta, KBW: Sought expense cadence and exit-rate clarity; CFO Van Denabeele said "2026 is a noisy year" and that "the bulk of those synergies will come after our systems conversion, which is at the end of July," while Q2 may tick up because "merit increases come online" before expenses "trend down throughout the year" (CFO Van Denabeele).
  • Andrew Terrell, Stephens: Questioned assumptions behind "$1" EPS and provision; CEO Laney replied, "our models will drive provisioning" and "there's nothing unusual" and said the path is "the strength of earning assets and fee income as well as realizing the expense synergies" (CEO Laney).
  • Matthew Clark, Piper Sandler: Asked about 2UniFi partnership progress; CEO Laney responded, "it remains a focus, and there's not much more we can say about it at this point" (CEO Laney).

Sentiment Analysis

  • Slightly negative analyst tone, centered on the mechanics behind the $1+ Q4 EPS claim and the realism of assumptions, including: "it kind of feels tough to get to $1 per share" (Andrew Terrell, Stephens) and questions about growth cadence and expense noise (KBW).
  • Slightly positive management tone with repeated confidence language, including: "we feel very, very good about our momentum" (CEO Laney) and "we remain confident in our trajectory towards achieving $1 EPS by the fourth quarter" (President Birkans).
  • Versus Q4 2025, management’s prepared remarks carried less emphasis on a “noisy” quarter and more emphasis on momentum and execution post-close; the Q4 2025 call repeatedly framed results as "noisy" and focused on cleanup actions, while Q1 2026 emphasized integration progress and near-term operating performance.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q1 2026 shifted from Q4 2025’s cleanup narrative ("It was a noisy fourth quarter") toward post-acquisition operating momentum, including "record quarterly loan originations of $805 million" and "net interest margin expanded...to 4.06%" (CFO Van Denabeele).
  • Guidance language was broadly consistent quarter-to-quarter: both calls reiterated ~10% 2026 loan growth and ~4% net interest margin framing, and both calls repeated the target to deliver "earnings in excess of $1 per share in the fourth quarter of 2026" (CFO Van Denabeele).
  • The Q4 2025 transcript highlighted potential variability from acquisition accounting ("we may recognize CECL day 1 provision expense"), while the Q1 2026 call emphasized realized progress on synergies and timing: "the majority...expected to be realized following the third quarter system integration" (CFO Van Denabeele).
  • Analyst focus moved from Q4 2025’s credit cleanup, payoffs, and 2UniFi partnership timing to Q1 2026’s sustainability of margin near 4%, cadence of expenses into the system conversion, and the feasibility/timing of hitting $1+ EPS.

Risks and Concerns

  • "For the remainder of 2026, we expect net interest margin to remain near 4%" (CFO Van Denabeele), with the outlook explicitly conditioned by: "our guidance does not include any future interest rate policy changes by the Fed" (CFO Van Denabeele).
  • "2026 is a noisy year on the expense front" with acquisition integration timing risk, as "the bulk of those synergies will come after our systems conversion, which is at the end of July" (CFO Van Denabeele).
  • 2UniFi execution risk was acknowledged by management: "we still have work to do to drive higher deposit account openings and loan fundings" (CEO Laney), and on partnership questions: "there's not much more we can say about it at this point" (CEO Laney).

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q1 2026 as an execution-driven quarter following the Vista close, highlighting $805 million of loan originations, a 4.06% net interest margin, and adjusted EPS of $0.72, while reiterating full-year 2026 targets around ~10% loan growth, net interest margin near 4%, fee income of $75 million to $80 million, and noninterest expense of $320 million to $330 million. In Q&A, analysts concentrated on the assumptions needed to reach “earnings in excess of $1 per share in the fourth quarter of 2026,” with management repeatedly tying that target to earning-asset growth, fee income, and post-conversion expense synergies, while acknowledging that 2UniFi continues to require improved conversion into deposit accounts and loan fundings.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
